title: Ferroelectric-tunable quantum nonlinearity of chiral Bloch electrons in a moiré system
authors: Zitian Pan, Jundong Zhu, Yu Hong, Jingwei Dong, Dongxia Shi, Kenji Watanabe, Takashi Taniguchi, Luojun Du, Wei Yang, Guangyu Zhang
abstract: Sliding ferroelectricity in van der Waals materials shows great potential for designing robust memory devices. However, its thermodynamic behaviors and the coupling with certain quantum effects remain largely unexplored. Here, we demonstrate ferroelectric control over quantum nonlinear transport in a hexagonal boron nitride (hBN) encapsulated twisted double-bilayer graphene moiré heterostructure. The ferroelectricity is attributed to the presence of rhombohedral stacking in the top hBN, confirmed by both electrical transport and optical second harmonic generation (SHG) measurements. Remarkably, the polarization magnitude remains temperature-independent across 1.7-200 K, while nucleation time exhibits thermally activated behavior, decreasing with increasing temperature. Furthermore, we demonstrate a ferroelectric-switchable nonlinear Hall effect, attributed to the chiral scattering induced by Berry curvature, with outstanding fatigue-resistant and nonvolatility, demonstrating direct coupling between sliding ferroelectricity and quantum geometric properties. Our results establish sliding ferroelectrics as a platform for exploring electrically programmable Berry curvature physics.
summary: This paper reports on ferroelectric control over quantum nonlinear transport in a moiré graphene heterostructure. By utilizing the ferroelectricity in the top hBN layer, the authors demonstrated a switchable nonlinear Hall effect linked to Berry curvature. This establishes the platform as a promising material system for electrically programmable quantum electronic devices.
main_problem: The study investigates the coupling between sliding ferroelectricity and quantum nonlinear transport in a moiré heterostructure, aiming to demonstrate ferroelectric control over quantum nonlinearities.
main_result: The authors demonstrated a ferroelectric-switchable nonlinear Hall effect, confirming a direct coupling between the ferroelectric polarization and the system's Berry curvature, which is robust and non-volatile.
method: The research combines electrical transport measurements (measuring $R_{xx}$ and nonlinear Hall response $\langle V_{xy}^{2\omega} angle$) with optical characterization ($	ext{SHG}$) and voltage pulse measurements to probe switching kinetics.
model_or_system: The physical platform is a hexagonal boron nitride ($	ext{hBN}$) encapsulated twisted double-bilayer graphene ($	ext{TDBG}$) moiré heterostructure, which exhibits ferroelectricity due to rhombohedral stacking in the top $	ext{hBN}$ layer.
key_observables: Nonlinear Hall response ($\langle V_{xy}^{2\omega} angle$), longitudinal resistance ($R_{xx}$), polarization magnitude ($P_{2D}$), and ferroelectric switching kinetics (nucleation time $t_c$).
important_parameters: Temperature range ($1.7-200	ext{ K}$), applied gate voltages ($V_t, V_b$), and excitation electric fields.
main_assumptions: The ferroelectricity is attributed specifically to the rhombohedral stacking in the top $	ext{hBN}$ layer, and the nonlinear effect is linked to chiral scattering induced by Berry curvature.
figures_summary: Figures detail $	ext{SHG}$ confirmation of ferroelectricity, $R_{xx}$ shifts under gate voltage sweeps, and hysteresis loops for the nonlinear Hall effect ($\langle V_{xy}^{2\omega} angle$) showing switching behavior under voltage pulses.
paper_structure: The paper systematically confirms the ferroelectric nature of the system, characterizes the temperature-independent polarization, and then demonstrates the ferroelectric-switchable nonlinear Hall effect, establishing the coupling mechanism.
why_interesting: This work provides an experimental realization of electrically programmable quantum geometric properties (Berry curvature) using ferroelectric switching, which is highly relevant for developing novel quantum electronic devices.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
